data.title: Smith Douglas homes expects 725-800 Q2 closings while holding pace over price strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Smith Douglas Homes (SDHC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Smith Douglas Homes reported a demand-and-incentives driven quarter in which management emphasized sales pace, quick cycle times, and expansion. CEO Greg Bennett said the company generated "$4.3 million in pretax income for the quarter" and "net income of $0.06 per share," while delivering "624 homes" and achieving "home closing gross margin" of "19.6% on a GAAP basis." Bennett also highlighted orders momentum, saying, "We generated 981 net new orders, up 28% from a year ago and a new quarterly record for the company," while describing order activity as "choppy" but improving sequentially through the quarter.

  • Bennett framed the operating strategy as prioritizing absorption and backlog quality, stating, "we remain focused on pace over price philosophy," with an emphasis on "driving towards a more presale oriented backlog." He reiterated operational execution, saying, "Our average build time was 57 days during the quarter," and tied competitive positioning to speed and customization: "our ability to deliver homes quickly and reliably with an offering of home choice and personalization" as "a key competitive advantage."

  • CFO Russ Devendorf anchored the quarter with revenue and margin drivers: "We finished the first quarter with $206.4 million in revenue on 624 closings" and "an average sales price of $331,000." He said GAAP gross margin was "19.6%" and adjusted gross margin was "20.3%," adding that margin benefited "by 170 basis points from the reduction of land development accruals on the closeout of several communities."

  • Devendorf also disclosed capital allocation action beginning in the quarter, stating, "During the first quarter, we began executing on our share repurchase authorization," and added, "Including repurchases completed in April, we have repurchased approximately $10 million of stock at an average price of $13.28 per share."

Outlook

  • Devendorf guided Q2 2026 to "closings between 725 and 800 homes," "average sales price between $325,000 and $330,000" and "gross margin between 17% and 17.5%." He said, "Given the continued variability in demand conditions, we are not providing full year guidance at this time."

  • Relative to the prior quarter’s call, management again tied the lack of full-year guidance to macro uncertainty; in Q4 2025, Devendorf used similar language: "Given the continued variability in demand conditions, we are not providing full year guidance at this time." In Q1 2026, he reiterated the same risk framing, saying the "primary risk" remains "macroeconomic conditions, including mortgage rates, consumer confidence and employment trends."

Financial Results

  • Devendorf attributed margin pressure to affordability tools and land basis, saying, "Our margins continue to reflect the use of incentives and targeted pricing adjustments to support affordability and maintain sales pace." He quantified buyer support costs: "closing costs, price discounts and the cost of forward commitments totaled 730 basis points," compared with "430 basis points in the year ago period" and "680 basis points sequentially from the fourth quarter of 2025."

  • On profitability and comparability, Devendorf reiterated the Up-C structure impact and provided adjusted profitability: "For the quarter, adjusted net income was $3.2 million compared to $14.7 million in the same period last year." He also detailed expense intensity: "Selling, general and administrative expenses for the quarter were $35.9 million or approximately 17.4% of revenue," which he said reflected growth-market investment and "the impact of lower average sales price."

  • Balance sheet metrics highlighted conservative leverage and liquidity, with Devendorf stating the company ended the quarter with "$28 million of cash" and "$68.5 million of total debt" and "approximately $195 million available" on the revolver, alongside "debt-to-book capitalization" of "13.6%" and "net debt to net book capitalization" of "8.5%."

Q&A

  • Nisarg Kalra, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: asked for detail on incentives/pricing and construction costs; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said Q1 margin included "170 basis points" of land development accrual reversals and that "the big driver still for us in kind of margin degradation is the lot cost," adding "lot costs were as a percentage of revenue, it's up about 300 basis points versus last year."

  • Nisarg Kalra, JPMorgan Chase & Co.: asked about early Q2 demand versus March; President, CEO & Vice Chairman Greg Bennett said April traffic was "a slight decline" and described it as "maybe down of 6% to 8% over what we were seeing earlier."

  • Stephen Mea, RBC Capital Markets: asked whether SG&A intensity should moderate as new divisions ramp; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said SG&A as a percentage of revenue "should definitely start to moderate" as volume ramps in Dallas, Chattanooga, and Gulf Coast, noting "we've got a lot of new fresh G&A that's hitting the books without any volume."

  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: asked about vertical cost outlook and supplier price increases; President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett said, "We've been pretty successful in pushing a lot of those increases off," adding, "the market is not allowing us price" and "we're holding a pretty tough line on that."

  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: asked about off-balance-sheet lots and land bank terms; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said "about 30% of our lots under option are with land bankers" and described "about a 10% deposit" with "another 10% walkaway fee" if the company exits, adding, "on all of our new land bank deals, we do not cross-collateralize."

  • Ryan Gilbert, BTIG: asked what drives Q2 margin step-down; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said incentives are assumed "about flat sequentially" while "lot cost is driving it," with additional variability tied to "how much are we able to hold on vertical costs."

  • Ryan Gilbert, BTIG: asked about current land prices and timing to flow through; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said land prices are "starting to turn" and "starting to flip from a seller's market to a buyer's market," but added it "takes 18 months to flow through" and that the company does not expect lot-cost pressure "to moderate for at least a couple of years" in a material way.

  • Natalie Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates: asked whether incentives rose into March; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said the 730 bps largely reflects items sold earlier, but as the company pushed "price discovery," incentives were "up a little bit," while adding the company was "pleasantly surprised" it "wasn't a huge hit."

  • Natalie Kulasekere, Zelman & Associates: asked about spec vs. presale mix and progress toward presales; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said the business has been about "40, 60 presale versus spec," but also cited improvement selling specs earlier, stating, "we're probably 70%, 80% before drywall stage has got a sale" and "our spec inventory has been coming down."

  • Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP Securities: asked whether smaller markets are seeing stronger demand than large metros; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said "Alabama has done really well," while President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett added, "the in-migration in some of the bigger metro locations we're in is down."

  • Jay McCanless, Citizens JMP Securities: asked about ARM usage; Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said the company shifted marketing to "a 3.99%, 5/1 ARM" from a "4.99%" 30-year fixed and that the "4.99%" is "probably taken the most"; SVP of Accounting & Finance Joe Thomas added, "And we're seeing more usage of the 4.99%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly negative, with repeated probing on margin durability, incentive intensity, land-cost inflation, and the visibility gap created by no full-year guidance (e.g., Ryan Gilbert asked about the Q2 margin step-down and land price relief timing; Nisarg Kalra pressed on incentives and demand choppiness).

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly positive on execution and demand responsiveness (e.g., President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett said, "We are encouraged by the price elasticity we experienced during the quarter"), while Q&A responses were more cautious and constraint-focused on affordability and land basis (e.g., Executive VP & CFO Devendorf: "the big driver still for us in kind of margin degradation is the lot cost").

  • Versus the prior quarter, the cadence sounded more execution-oriented around sales improvement and backlog rebuilding, while maintaining similar caution on macro variability; both quarters used consistent language around week-to-week inconsistency and the decision not to provide full-year guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized a sales rebound and community expansion, with President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett highlighting "981 net new orders" and "108 active communities," compared with Q4 2025 commentary that sales conditions "remain choppy" and the company "chose to remain disciplined" amid "aggressive year-end discounting from some competitors."

  • The operating philosophy remained consistent across calls, with both quarters reiterating "pace over price" and willingness to accept near-term margin pressure to protect volume and inventory turns; in Q1 2026, Executive VP & CFO Devendorf reiterated, "our focus remains on maintaining absorption and inventory turns even if that requires some pressure on margins in the short term."

  • Capital allocation moved from intent to execution: in Q4 2025, Devendorf said the company saw "an opportunity to opportunistically repurchase shares"; in Q1 2026, he reported repurchases totaling "approximately $10 million" including April activity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management repeatedly tied near-term performance to affordability and macro drivers. Executive VP & CFO Devendorf said the market remains "driven primarily by affordability pressures and elevated mortgage rates" and cited uncertainty from "mixed" economic data and "geopolitical developments," adding the company is "monitoring labor market trends closely."

  • Cost structure risks centered on land basis and potential fuel-related impacts. Devendorf said lot costs are the key margin headwind, while President, CEO & Vice Chairman Bennett warned that if higher fuel persists, "we're going to get hit with fuel surcharges."

Final Takeaway

Smith Douglas Homes’ management described Q1 2026 as a pace-driven quarter, pairing record quarterly net orders with continued reliance on financing tools and targeted pricing to address affordability. The company guided Q2 closings to 725-800 with gross margin of 17%-17.5% and reiterated that it will not provide full-year guidance due to variable demand and macro risks. Executives repeatedly pointed to lot-cost inflation as the primary margin headwind, said land pricing is beginning to moderate but will take time to flow through, and disclosed that share repurchases have begun, totaling about $10 million including April.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Apple plans to add Siri mode to camera app and new visual AI tools to iOS 27: report
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) plans to add more artificial intelligence features to the iOS 27 operating system, including a Siri mode inside its camera app along with other new AI visual features, according to Bloomberg.

The company's Visual Intelligence feature will be located within the camera app, the report said. It will appear as a new toggle option. The updates are part of Apple's broader efforts to incorporate AI and will likely be revealed at its Worldwide Developers Conference in June.

These updates to Apple's visual AI capabilities will likely play a role in new devices the company is reportedly working on, such as smart glasses and a pendant. They will allow Siri to scan environments and provide details back to the user.

Apple is also developing an updated version of its Photos app. These include new AI-powered tools for editing and enhancing images, the report said. These will likely be ready for the release of the iPhone 18 series when it's released in September.

data.title: Amazon earnings call preview: AI, tariffs expected to dominate discussion
data.description:
Amazon (AMZN) is scheduled to report first-quarter results after Wednesday’s close, with a conference call set for 5:30 p.m. ET. Prediction market traders on Kalshi are pricing in an earnings discussion heavy on tariffs and artificial intelligence themes.

According to Kalshi data, “tariff” mentions are priced at 96% probability, with “live sports” at 95%. References to “Alexa+ / Alexa Plus” and “silicon” both sit at 94%, while “robotics” comes in at 93%. “Data center” and “Rufus” are each priced at 92%, with “OpenAI” at 90%.

Lower-probability mentions include “supply chain” at 57% and “China” at 32%, suggesting traders view tariff discussion as nearly certain but are less convinced management will explicitly frame pressures around China or logistics specifically.

The prediction market setup effectively maps Amazon’s (AMZN) current investor narrative: tariff exposure in its retail business, AI spending through Amazon Web Services and data centers, in-house chip development, automation and consumer-facing AI products.

When the company posts its quarterly results before the call, investors will be closely watching the performance of its cloud division and looking for signs that the tech giant’s heavy investment in AI has started to deliver tangible results. Wall Street expects the e-commerce giant to post EPS of $1.65 on revenue of $177.16B.

data.title: Verisk reaffirms 2026 adjusted EPS of $7.45-$7.75 while targeting gradual growth improvement after Q1 trough
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Verisk Analytics (VRSK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Lee Shavel said Verisk delivered “organic constant currency revenue growth of 4.7%” and “sustained strong growth of 7% in subscription revenues,” while “our focus on efficiency and cost discipline drove organic constant currency adjusted EBITDA growth of 5.9%, delivering 60 basis points of margin expansion.” (CEO, President & Director Lee Shavel)

  • Shavel framed near-term headwinds as temporary, saying results included “the carryover impact of the very low weather activity, tougher compares from strong renewals last year and a work stoppage in a federal government contract,” while adding, “we have confidence that the resolution of these short-term factors and continued core growth momentum will result in a gradual improvement in revenue growth as we move through the year.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Shavel highlighted strategic emphasis from Investor Day: “drive compounding growth by focusing on 4 key initiatives, specifically strengthening strategic client relationships, expanding our proprietary and contributory data advantage, delivering a steady stream of innovations to the market and expanding networks across our businesses.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • On product momentum, Shavel said enhanced aerial imagery offerings “growing revenue more than 30% over the last 2 years,” with “wind and hail peril scores and remaining roof life” slated for introduction this year. (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Shavel emphasized anti-fraud AI adoption: “just this quarter, we onboarded the sixth top 10 carrier to the Digital Media Forensics platform.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • CFO Elizabeth Mann reported GAAP results: “first quarter revenue was $783 million,” “net income was $234 million,” and “diluted GAAP earnings per share were $1.73.” (Executive VP & CFO Elizabeth Mann)

Outlook

  • Mann reaffirmed full-year guidance: “consolidated revenue in the range of $3.19 billion to $3.24 billion,” “adjusted EBITDA… between $1.79 billion and $1.83 billion with adjusted EBITDA margins of 56% to 56.5%,” and “adjusted earnings per share for the year in the range of $7.45 to $7.75.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Mann reiterated growth pacing language from last quarter, stating, “we expect the first quarter of 2026 to be a trough, both in terms of organic constant currency revenue growth rates and absolute dollars and forecast a gradual improvement as we move through the year.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • On comparables and notable items embedded in guidance, Mann said, “we continue to face tougher comparisons in the first half,” guidance reflects the Verisk Marketing Solutions divestiture, which “represents an $0.11 headwind to earnings per share,” and “the prior year quarter’s reported margin benefited from a foreign currency translation impact… 120 basis points… which we do not expect to recur.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

Financial Results

  • Mann detailed segment performance on an organic constant currency basis: “OCC revenues grew 4.7% with growth of 5.3% in underwriting and 3.4% in claims,” while “subscription revenues, which comprised 84% of our total revenues in the quarter, grew 7%.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Mann described mixed revenue type performance: “transactional revenues… comprised 16% of our total revenues… declined 6.1%,” citing “lower volumes in Property and Restoration Solutions due to low levels of weather activity,” plus “softness in the personal lines auto business.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Mann said profitability reflected leverage and investment: “total adjusted EBITDA margins… were 55.9%, up 60 basis points,” driven by “global talent optimization, offset in part by increased investment in AI technology.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Mann updated capital structure and interest expense drivers: “net interest expense was $43 million,” and “we issued $1 billion of senior notes and entered into a $500 million term loan” to fund “the… $1.5 billion accelerated share repurchase program,” with leverage at “2.4x debt to adjusted EBITDA.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • On cash returns, Mann said Verisk paid “a cash dividend of $0.50 per share,” initiated the “$1.5 billion accelerated share repurchase program,” and “in total, we retired 7.6 million shares in the first quarter of ’26.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

Q&A

  • Ashish Sabadra, RBC Capital Markets: asked about renewal pricing; CEO Shavel said large-client multiyear renewals achieved “stronger price increases,” with contract terms “averaging approximately between 4 and 5 years,” and annual increases “reflective of the greater value… through our Core Lines reimagine initiative.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley: asked about AI/data monetization; Shavel said monetization is “rooted” in delivering “structured, clean industry-wide” datasets, with strategy including “realizing more value through our pricing arrangements” and enabling “new specific data utilization for AI applications.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Jeffrey Meuler, Baird: asked if extended AI sales cycles imply added caution; Shavel said AI adds “additional complexity” around “intellectual property… privacy,” and “yes, there is a little bit of caution,” tied to contract work even as the pipeline grows. (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs: asked about cadence of improvement; CFO Mann said “we expected 1Q to be the trough,” and “the second quarter” may remain pressured on an OCC basis due to persisting headwinds, before “the underlying strength and health of the business… reemerge.” (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Kelsey Zhu, Autonomous: asked about AI sizing and margin impact; Mann said, “we haven’t sized it on the top line,” and “for the time being, we see it as a push on the margin,” while noting productivity benefits and continued investment. (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on durability and execution timing, including “extended sales cycles” (Meuler, Baird) and whether subscription growth in the “high single-digit range” is sustainable (Scott Wurtzel, Wolfe Research).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and occasionally cautious in Q&A, emphasizing confidence while acknowledging friction points: Shavel said “yes, there is a little bit of caution” tied to AI contracting complexity, while Mann reiterated “we expected 1Q to be the trough.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel; Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Versus last quarter’s more expansive AI framing, this quarter added more explicit commercialization detail (e.g., “sixth top 10 carrier” onboarded) alongside clearer acknowledgement of contracting delays. (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance levels and core pacing language were consistent with Q4 2025, with Mann again describing Q1 as “the trough” and reiterating the same 2026 ranges for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted EPS. (Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Management’s strategic narrative shifted from Q4 portfolio actions (AccuLynx termination and Verisk Marketing Solutions sale) to Q1 execution against Investor Day initiatives and AI commercialization, including a “competitive RFP” win to co-develop “a next-generation digitally native underwriting entity.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

  • Analysts’ questions remained heavily AI- and pricing-focused both quarters, but Q1 featured more probing on monetization models and contracting friction, while Q4 included more discussion of transactional revenue recovery and competitive dynamics in personal auto. (CEO, President & Director Shavel; Executive VP & CFO Mann)

Risks and Concerns

  • Shavel and Mann cited near-term headwinds: “very low weather activity,” “tougher compares,” and “a work stoppage in a federal government contract,” with Mann noting transactional pressure from “lower volumes” in property/restoration and “softness” in personal auto. (CEO, President & Director Shavel; Executive VP & CFO Mann)

  • Management flagged AI go-to-market friction as a risk to timing, as Shavel described longer negotiations due to “intellectual property… privacy issues,” and said resolution should improve “over time” as “industry standards… improve.” (CEO, President & Director Shavel)

Final Takeaway

Verisk management described Q1 as a temporary “trough” quarter driven by low weather-related volumes, tougher subscription renewal compares, and a government-contract work stoppage, while reaffirming full-year 2026 targets of $3.19 billion-$3.24 billion revenue and $7.45-$7.75 adjusted EPS. Leadership emphasized pricing realization in multiyear renewals, continued margin discipline alongside AI investment, accelerating AI product adoption (including onboarding a “sixth top 10 carrier” to Digital Media Forensics), and acknowledged that AI governance and IP considerations are extending certain sales cycles even as pipelines and proof-of-concept activity increase.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Etsy anticipates Q2 GMS of $2.48B-$2.53B while keeping adjusted EBITDA margin at 27%-29%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Etsy (ETSY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "I stepped into my first quarter as CEO after more than 15 years at Etsy" and said "all of our key performance indicators came in at or ahead of our expectations with GMS at $2.5 billion, up 5.5% year-over-year for the Etsy Marketplace" alongside "revenue of $631 million" and "adjusted EBITDA of $185 million or 29.3% adjusted EBITDA margin" (CEO, President & Director Kruti Goyal).
  • "Active buyers grew sequentially for the first time in 2 years" and "GMS per active buyer grew year-over-year for the first time since 2022" as management emphasized the app as "the centerpiece of this transformation" with "App GMS... now makes up about 47% of total GMS" (CEO Goyal).
  • "On February 15, we entered into an agreement to sell Depop to eBay for $1.2 billion" and "we expect to close the transaction by the end of the third quarter of 2026" as results are presented on a continuing operations basis with Depop in discontinued operations (Chief Financial Officer Charles Baker).

Outlook

  • "We currently anticipate that Etsy Marketplace second quarter GMS will be between $2.48 billion and $2.53 billion, representing year-over-year growth of approximately 3% to 5%" and "we expect second quarter take rate to be approximately 25.7% and adjusted EBITDA margin to be 27% to 29%" (CFO Baker).
  • "For the full year, we now anticipate that GMS growth will be in the low single-digit range" while "our full year adjusted EBITDA margin outlook of 28% to 30% remains unchanged" (CFO Baker).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management changed its full-year framing from "achieving slight growth for 2026" (Q4 2025) to "GMS growth will be in the low single-digit range" (Q1 2026), and also said the outlook "has improved relative to the full year commentary provided in mid-February" (CFO Baker).

Financial Results

  • "In the first quarter" Etsy reported "GMS at $2.5 billion" for the Etsy Marketplace, "revenue of $631 million" and "adjusted EBITDA of $185 million" with "29.3% adjusted EBITDA margin" and a "take rate of 25.7%" (CEO Goyal).
  • "Q1 '26 trailing 12-month active buyer count was 86.6 million" with "combined gross buyer additions... 11.9 million" and "GMS per active buyer... reaching $122 on a trailing 12-month basis" while "active sellers grew 3.3% to 5.6 million" (CFO Baker).
  • "Etsy held $1.6 billion in cash, cash equivalents and short- and long-term investments" and reported "net cash provided by operating activities of continuing operations was $102.5 million" plus "we also repurchased a total of $145 million of stock" and "we have $828 million remaining" on authorized repurchase programs (CFO Baker).

Q&A

  • Michael Morton, MoffettNathanson: asked whether app changes are driving frequency; CEO Goyal: "We're not seeing it inflected yet" and said app-home work is "delivering deeper engagement and more engagement" as "the precursor to greater frequency."
  • Trevor Young, Barclays: asked about the multi-year path for take rate and whether Etsy could add services or reduce friction; CEO Goyal: "our focus is very much on growing revenue through GMS growth" and "we expect to see some modest improvement in take rate" as seller services improve.
  • Maria Ripps, Canaccord: asked about active buyer growth drivers and lag; CFO Baker: "there really are 2 components" and said growth depends on "bring[ing] in more new buyers and reactivate more prior buyers" with mix shifts including "showing up much more strongly in places like social media" and "leaning out on... agentic commerce."
  • Nicholas Jones, BNP Paribas: asked about AI maturity and token/compute expense; CEO Goyal: "we're still really early days" while CFO Baker said costs will be managed with "a very rigorous ROI discipline" tied to "impact on GMS growth."
  • Marvin Fong, BTIG: asked about consumer health, fuel prices, and postage surcharges; CFO Baker: "the consumer environment has remained relatively stable overall" and said it is "hard to point to any one thing" while management remains "cautious about the forward outlook."
  • Bryan Smilek, JPMorgan: asked about OpenAI’s shift and Etsy’s agentic impact; CEO Goyal: "Just want to clarify the app has not launched yet" and said agentic traffic is "a fraction of a percent of our total traffic" and primarily a "discovery" channel.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly skeptical to slightly negative, pressing on frequency timing, non-app performance, AI cost/ROI, take rate durability, and macro sensitivity, including "the obligatory question about consumer health" (Marvin Fong, BTIG).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A, using phrases like "We're not seeing it inflected yet" and "we remain cautious about the forward outlook" while repeatedly emphasizing "early" progress in AI and frequency initiatives (CEO Goyal; CFO Baker).
  • Versus last quarter, management sounded more operationally grounded in Q&A, reiterating that progress "won't always be linear" (CEO Goyal) compared with Q4’s heavier emphasis on diagnosing issues and resetting priorities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Process and messaging shifted from Q4’s slide-deck framing to a "shareholder letter" and "shortened our prepared remarks" to expand Q&A time (Vice President of Investor Relations Debra Wasser).
  • The Depop sale moved from announcement and expected close "in the second quarter of 2026" (Q4 2025) to an update that reviews are "on track" and closing is expected "by the end of the third quarter of 2026" (Q1 2026) (CFO Baker).
  • App emphasis intensified, with management moving from Q4 app contribution "reached 46%" to Q1 app share "about 47% of total GMS" and highlighting faster app GMS growth (CEO Goyal).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Our progress won't always be linear" and the company flagged "high macro uncertainty" while monitoring "consumer confidence" (CEO Goyal).
  • CFO Baker warned that "prior year comparisons will become less favorable as we move through the year" and that Q1 benefited from factors expected to moderate, including foreign exchange tailwinds and AOV-related effects tied to "the expiration of the de minimis tariff exemption" (CFO Baker).
  • On AI/agentic commerce, management repeatedly stressed "very, very early" adoption with current traffic "a fraction of a percent" while positioning experimentation under ROI discipline (CEO Goyal; CFO Baker).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as an "encouraging start" with Etsy Marketplace GMS at $2.5 billion, a 25.7% take rate, and sequential active buyer growth for the first time in two years, while attributing early momentum to app-led personalization, improved discovery and matching, and more effective marketing mix shifts. For Q2, leadership guided to Etsy Marketplace GMS of $2.48 billion to $2.53 billion with take rate around 25.7% and adjusted EBITDA margin of 27% to 29%, and updated full-year expectations to low single-digit GMS growth with adjusted EBITDA margin unchanged at 28% to 30%, while also highlighting the pending Depop divestiture expected to close by the end of Q3 2026 and the intent to accelerate shareholder returns via repurchases.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Brown-Forman drops after Pernod Ricard talks end, JPMorgan downgrades
data.description:
Brown-Forman (BF. A) (BF. B) plunged 10% after deal talks with Pernod Ricard (PDRDF) ended without a transaction. The maker of Jack Daniel's whiskey was also downgraded to underweight from neutral at JPMorgan.

Pernod Ricard (PDRDF) and Brown-Forman (BF.B) said that discussions have ended as the companies were unable to reach mutually acceptable terms, the companies said in separate statements on Tuesday.

While some investors were hoping that a rival bid from Sazerac for Brown-Forman (BF.B) may still be in the cards, JPMorgan downplayed that idea.  

"With less strategic fit, potentially more burdensome regulatory process, and likely less control vs. a RI deal, we view a takeover by Sazerac as lower probability, particularly with BF press release wording noting focus on internal initiatives," JPMorgan analyst Drew Levine wrote in a note on Tuesday. 

Levine also cut his price target on Brown-Forman (BF.B) to $23 from $27. 

Brown-Forman (BF.B) appeared to indicate in its statement on Tuesday that it was focused on its strategy as a standalone business.

 “We intend to create long-term value for all stakeholders by focusing on our strategic and operational priorities," Brown-Forman said in a statement. "This includes unlocking future growth by expanding our geographic footprint, continuing to build brands that resonate with consumers, and enhancing operational efficiency.”

data.title: Prog holdings signals 2026 outlook of $3.0B-$3.1B revenue and $4.40-$4.80 non-GAAP EPS as leverage returns to 2x
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PROG Holdings (PRG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered a strong first quarter" and "our results came in at the high end of our revenue outlook and exceeded the top end of our outlook for earnings and non-GAAP EPS," driven by "higher-than-expected GMV with improved economics at Four" and "better portfolio yield at Progressive Leasing" from "lower-than-expected utilization of 90-day purchase options" (CEO, President & Director Steven Michaels).

  • "Consolidated GMV, which grew 54% in Q1" was highlighted as the company "begun framing growth through the lens of consolidated GMV" as leasing, Four, and Purchasing Power contribute to platform scale (CEO, President & Director Michaels).

  • Leasing trends were described as improving through the quarter: "January down high single digits, February down low single digits and March up low single digits" year-over-year, with Michaels saying the company was "excited to exit the quarter on a growth trajectory."

  • Four was positioned as both a growth and profitability driver: "Four's GMV for the quarter was 134% higher year-over-year" and "we are seeing that growth translate into attractive economics and profitability" (CEO, President & Director Michaels).

  • Purchasing Power integration and growth were emphasized: "Purchasing Power's Q1 GMV grew double digits at 10.3%" and the company "added several new employer clients during the quarter, bringing tens of thousands of new eligible employees onto the platform" (CEO, President & Director Michaels).

  • "Our revised consolidated outlook for continuing operations for 2026 calls for revenues in the range of $3 billion to $3.1 billion, adjusted EBITDA in the range of $343 million to $370 million and non-GAAP EPS in the range of $4.40 to $4.80" (Chief Financial Officer Brian Garner).

Outlook

  • "Our revised consolidated outlook for 2026 raises expectations on both the revenue and earnings from continuing operations" and is based on assumptions including "no meaningful increase in the unemployment rates for our customer base" and "no impact from additional share repurchases" (CFO Garner).

  • On leasing credit, "we expect full year 2026 lease merchandise write-offs to remain within our targeted annual range of 6% to 8%" (CFO Garner).

  • On Four profitability seasonality, "Q1 is the strongest margin period for Four, and throughout the remainder of the year, I expect margins to moderate to the range implied in the revised outlook for the segment" (CFO Garner).

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s 2026 view, management moved from "non-GAAP EPS in the range of $4 and $4.45" to "$4.40 to $4.80" while keeping revenues at "$3 billion to $3.1 billion" (CFO Garner, Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).

Financial Results

  • "Revenue from continuing operations grew 11.1% year-over-year to $742.7 million" and "consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $90.3 million" with "non-GAAP diluted EPS" of "$1.24" (CFO Garner).

  • Segment specifics included: "First quarter GMV came in at $393 million" for Progressive Leasing with segment revenue of "$597 million" and "gross margin" of "31.5%"; Four revenue of "$35 million" and "adjusted EBITDA was $12.9 million"; Purchasing Power revenue of "$107.1 million" and "adjusted EBITDA of $0.8 million" (CFO Garner).

  • Balance sheet and leverage were a focal point: "we paid down recourse debt by $210 million" and "ended the quarter with $650 million of recourse debt" alongside a "net leverage ratio of 2x" (CFO Garner).

Q&A

  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens Inc., Research Division: asked about macro impacts across products; CEO Michaels said the macro impact is "directionally similar across the products," tax season "played out about as we expected," and "gas prices during the month of March became a bigger story" while the company is "watching all of our early indicators intensely."

  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens Inc., Research Division: asked about retail partner discussions in a tough environment; CEO Michaels said the team has "a very, very good pipeline of retailers of all sizes" and described "increased conversations about a multiple product solution with various retailers."

  • Robert Griffin, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked what lower 90-day buyouts may signal; CEO Michaels said 90-day behavior in 2023 "did not indicate" elevated charge-offs and that the company is "pleased with the roll rates," while CFO Garner added the shift toward lower 90-days is expected to be "a net positive for the P&L over the course of the year."

  • Robert Griffin, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked whether leasing GMV inflection was comps or true improvement; CEO Michaels said there was "a mix" of lapping headwinds and "some strength in our digital channels," citing "marketplace being up again 169%" and e-commerce GMV mix "25.7% of total Progressive Leasing GMV."

  • Hoang Nguyen, TD Cowen, Research Division: asked about cross-selling between leasing and Purchasing Power; CEO Michaels said it was "definitely part of our plan" and noted discussions with retailer-employers are "underway" but "we're very early in the integration."

  • Hoang Nguyen, TD Cowen, Research Division: asked about timing of buybacks now that leverage is in range; CFO Garner said the company is "sizing up that fourth quarter" cash need and will consider repurchases "to the extent that we have excess capital."

  • Anthony Chukumba, Loop Capital Markets LLC, Research Division: asked why Four’s margin outlook improved; CEO Michaels said it is "largely due to scale" and also "an efficiency play" as Four "leaned into AI" with "back-office savings" and "lack of churn" as a bright spot.

  • Harold Goetsch, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: asked about new income statement items and fair value changes; CEO Michaels said selling "aged lease receivables" is "not a onetime thing" though "probably not" at the "same quantum," and CFO Garner said the Purchasing Power fair value line should be "slight adjustments each quarter" and "not... material."

  • Bradley Thomas, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: asked about leasing GMV confidence and when revenue could flip; CEO Michaels said the leasing revenue guide implies "similar trends" continuing, and CFO Garner pointed to portfolio size improving from "down 9.4%" entering the quarter to "down 5.4%" at quarter-end, adding "I don't think it's too far down the road" before portfolio size grows year-over-year.

  • Bradley Thomas, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: asked for free cash flow guidance and debt paydown; CFO Garner said "We haven't provided free cash flow guidance" and described Q4 as likely "a net cash need" given seasonal GMV and cash usage.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated framing such as "Congrats on a really strong start" and multiple questions probing sustainability of leasing GMV inflection, Four margin durability, leverage-driven buybacks, and modeling items tied to Purchasing Power accounting (Joseph, Griffin, Nguyen, Chukumba, Goetsch, Thomas).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks ("very happy with the start to the year" and "real momentum") and more measured in Q&A on macro, emphasizing monitoring and discipline: "we're watching all of our early indicators intensely" and "we're taking it month by month" (CEO, President & Director Michaels; CFO Garner).

  • Versus last quarter, management’s language shifted from planning for a "challenging" environment with "higher expected tax refunds" (Q4) to stressing "gas prices are elevated" and "increased uncertainty" while still stating "we feel good about where we're positioned" (CEO, President & Director Michaels; Q4 2025 vs. Q1 2026).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance moved higher for 2026 profitability metrics, with CFO Garner updating to "adjusted EBITDA in the range of $343 million to $370 million" and "non-GAAP EPS" of "$4.40 to $4.80" versus Q4 guidance of "$320 million to $350 million" and "$4 and $4.45" (CFO Garner).

  • Strategic emphasis stayed consistent on the ecosystem and consolidated GMV, but the current call added more specificity on Four profitability ("adjusted EBITDA of $12.9 million" in Q1) and on deleveraging execution ("paid down $210 million" to reach "2x") (CEO, President & Director Michaels; CFO Garner).

  • Analyst focus shifted from initial Purchasing Power seasonality and integration framing (Q4) toward capital return timing, accounting line items, and whether leasing GMV and portfolio size are at an inflection point (Q1) (Joseph, Goetsch, Thomas).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macro pressure on the core consumer: "Gas prices are elevated, and there is increased uncertainty in the macro backdrop" (CEO, President & Director Michaels).

  • Leasing demand risk remained tied to discretionary retail: "consumer demand across several discretionary categories remains pressured" and Progressive Leasing revenue headwinds were linked to "a smaller average lease portfolio" (CFO Garner).

  • Capital allocation was framed around seasonal cash needs and leverage: CFO Garner said Q4 could be "a net cash need" and that share repurchases depend on "excess capital" after business investment and liquidity planning.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as an above-plan start driven by improving Progressive Leasing trends, continued triple-digit expansion at Four with profitability, and early Purchasing Power progress, while executing rapid deleveraging to a 2x net leverage ratio. The company raised 2026 earnings expectations to $343M-$370M adjusted EBITDA and $4.40-$4.80 non-GAAP EPS, while reiterating that macro uncertainty—especially elevated gas prices—keeps portfolio monitoring, cost discipline, and seasonal cash planning central to execution through the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: PPG reaffirms 2026 EPS $7.70-$8.10 as it targets offsetting mid-single-digit COGS inflation with low single-digit pricing
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PPG Industries (PPG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "I am pleased to report that PPG delivered solid performance in the first quarter, demonstrating our ability to maintain growth momentum in a challenging macro environment, led by our differentiated aerospace and PPG Comex businesses," said (CEO & Chairman Timothy Knavish).

  • "We achieved organic sales growth of positive 1%," and "First quarter net sales totaled $3.9 billion" with "adjusted earnings per share of $1.83" and "Our segment EBITDA margin was over 19%," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish).

  • "With the impact of the Iran war, costs have risen for raw materials, energy, logistics and packaging across the coatings value chain," and "the impact of PPG is expected to be a mid-single-digit percentage in the cost of goods sold for the remainder of the year," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish). He added, "We expect to fully offset these costs, and we are proactively raising prices to secure raw materials for our customers."

  • "We are reaffirming our full year 2026 EPS guidance range of $7.70 to $8.10," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish). He also highlighted capital returns and liquidity: "We ended the quarter with cash and short-term investments of about $1.6 billion" and "returned approximately $260 million to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases."

  • "Our backlog remains at about $315 million" in aerospace, said (CEO & Chairman Knavish), while also noting capacity investments: "Round numbers over the last year or so, we've put about $150 million into those kind of investments" and "we announced a new plant to the tune of about $380 million."

  • "The currency impact for Q1 was less than $0.10 year-over-year positive," said (Senior VP & CFO Vincent Morales), adding, "If you look at the balance of the year, so the remaining 3 quarters, the total is going to be less than half of that and most of that in Q2."

  • "I would like to congratulate Vince on his upcoming retirement on this, his final PPG earnings call," and "we are delighted to welcome Jamie Beggs as our new Chief Financial Officer," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish).

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter of 2026... This will result in organic sales growth for the second quarter in the range of flat to positive low single digits versus the prior year," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish), while also stating, "we expect overall pricing for the company to be positive" with "flat year-over-year price in the Industrial Coatings segments."

  • "We expect to deliver adjusted earnings per share growth in the range of flat to a positive low single-digit percentage for the second quarter versus the prior year period," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish).

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing of "expected overall flat raw material costs for the year" (Q4 call), management in Q1 said, "the impact of PPG is expected to be a mid-single-digit percentage in the cost of goods sold for the remainder of the year," while also reaffirming the full-year EPS range.

Financial Results

  • "First quarter net sales totaled $3.9 billion" and "adjusted earnings per share of $1.83," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish). He also reported segment-level sales: Global Architectural Coatings "net sales rose 13% to $965 million," Performance Coatings "5% positive net sales growth to $1.3 billion," and Industrial Coatings "net sales grew 4% to $1.6 billion."

  • On capital allocation and balance sheet actions, (CEO & Chairman Knavish) said, "We repaid $700 million of debt that matured in the first quarter" and "returned approximately $260 million to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases."

  • On cash flow seasonality and 2026 cash priorities, (Senior VP & CFO Morales) said, "If you look at our cash from ops, we were up about $50 million versus the prior year" and "our cash forecast do not change versus what we gave in January." (CEO & Chairman Knavish) added, "we expect a good proxy walking around number for us is for our cash flow to be about 10% of our sales."

Q&A

  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird: asked how PPG expects "price cost recovery" to be faster and whether volumes can hold given "near 20% increases"; (CEO & Chairman Knavish) responded, "Now we're talking months" and said PPG has "tremendous momentum from an organic growth standpoint" and is "confident that we're going to be able to strike the right balance between pricing and volume."

  • Michael Sison, Wells Fargo: asked about second-half volumes and Iran conflict impacts; (CEO & Chairman Knavish) said, "based on today's environment, we feel good about the second half volume" and "we have not seen any order book changes with the Iran conflict." (Senior VP & CFO Morales) added, "we still expect muted volumes in refinish for the year" due to comparisons.

  • David Begleiter, Deutsche Bank: asked about realization and durability of "up to 20%" price increases; (CEO & Chairman Knavish) said "the actual realization will be spread out" and that PPG needs "low single digits to offset" inflation, adding, "there will be a lag coming down" if inputs fall.

  • Jeffrey Zekauskas, JPMorgan: asked about FX to EBIT and pricing recovery in auto OEM, plus aerospace capacity; (Senior VP & CFO Morales) said, "The currency impact for Q1 was less than $0.10 year-over-year positive." (CEO & Chairman Knavish) said auto OEM is "the toughest... to get pricing" but index contracts "will automatically move with some time lag," and on aerospace: "you will see increases in output volume and therefore, revenue" and cited a "$380 million" new plant.

  • Vincent Andrews, Morgan Stanley: questioned Industrial Coatings margin softness; (CEO & Chairman Knavish) cited "automotive OEM builds in China" and "index contracts" rolling off, adding, "We should be wrapping up the roll-off of those in Q2." (Senior VP & CFO Morales) said, "On a 2-year stack basis, we're almost flat in China."

  • Patrick Cunningham, Citi: asked about Europe architectural plant closures; (CEO & Chairman Knavish) said, "a good walking around number is about a $25 million reduction in our fixed cost base from the closure of those 4 plants" and "you'll see about a $50 million structural restructuring benefits for our company this year" and "another $50 million next year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on inflation pass-through and end-market risk, with questions probing competitiveness and cost credibility, including Laurent Favre, BNP Paribas pointing to "spot prices... at more than 50%, sometimes 100%" and asking "how it's only MSD?"

  • Management tone was slightly positive and action-oriented in prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and offset plans, including (CEO & Chairman Knavish) saying, "We expect to fully offset these costs" and "We are confident in our strategy," while in Q&A repeatedly time-stamping uncertainty, stating, "you kind of have to time stamp right now because it's just so fluid out there."

  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1 included more defensive clarification on inflation mechanics and contracts (surcharges, index lag), while Q4 focused more on favorable raw materials and growth momentum.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 introduced a new macro driver—"the impact of the Iran war"—and shifted raw material commentary from Q4’s "expected overall flat raw material costs for the year" to Q1’s "mid-single-digit percentage" COGS inflation expectation, alongside "proactively raising prices" and citing price increases "up to 20%."

  • Q1 added a specific Europe structural action: "4 manufacturing plants that will be closed in the second half of 2026" with a cited "$25 million" fixed-cost reduction, extending Q4’s broader emphasis on self-help.

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s operating leverage/corporate costs and long-cycle AI discussions toward Q1’s speed of price-cost recovery, durability of price, industrial margin sensitivity (China mix/index), and Iran-related supply chain and demand risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Costs have risen for raw materials, energy, logistics and packaging" tied to the Iran war, while PPG’s mitigation plan was "proactively raising prices" and leveraging "our unique broad and global supply chain footprint," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish).

  • Europe architectural demand remained weak and "mixed by country" with a "low single-digit percentage decline," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish), while mitigation included "reduce our overall structural costs" and plant closures.

  • Refinish remained pressured near term: "we expect year-over-year organic sales volume declines in the second quarter" and "We anticipate volume growth during the second half of 2026," said (CEO & Chairman Knavish).

Final Takeaway

PPG management described Q1 as "solid performance" with organic sales growth of "positive 1%" and reaffirmed full-year 2026 EPS guidance of $7.70 to $8.10, while repositioning the near-term narrative around Iran-war-driven inflation that is expected to lift costs by a "mid-single-digit percentage" of COGS. Leadership emphasized rapid price actions (including increases announced "up to 20%"), capacity-driven aerospace growth with a $315 million backlog and a $380 million new plant, and additional self-help in Europe including four plant closures targeted to reduce fixed costs by about $25 million.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Stanley Black & Decker targets $4.90-$5.70 EPS in 2026 while planning $500M in share repurchases
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "I am pleased to report that Stanley Black & Decker delivered a solid start to the year, outperforming our expectations on the top and bottom lines in the first quarter" (President, CEO & Director Christopher Nelson), while reporting revenue up 3% (flat organically) and adjusted EPS of $0.80.

  • Nelson highlighted the CAM divestiture close and capital priorities, saying, "on April 6, we announced the successful completion of the previously disclosed agreement to sell our Aerospace Fasteners business" and that "the vast majority of the approximately $1.6 billion of net proceeds have already been applied towards debt reduction" (President, CEO & Director Nelson), adding, "We expect our capital allocation strategy to be biased towards share repurchases, which the Board has authorized."

  • On Tools & Outdoor, management tied mixed volumes to North American retail conditions and outdoor preseason execution. Nelson said organic revenue declined in power tools and hand tools/storage, while "Outdoor organic revenue increased 1%, driven by encouraging preseason sales for spring 2026, particularly for ride-on and zero-turn mower offerings" (President, CEO & Director Nelson). He also pointed to professional demand, stating, "increased sales generated by professional end user demand in the U.S. commercial and industrial channel indicates that our growth investments are building momentum in the market" (President, CEO & Director Nelson).

  • "Above-the-line operating outperformance made up about half of the outperformance, driven by Outdoor" (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Patrick Hallinan), and Hallinan attributed the remaining outperformance to below-the-line items, including a Q1 tax rate benefit that he said did not change the full-year tax rate view.

Outlook

  • Management kept full-year adjusted EPS guidance unchanged at $4.90 to $5.70, with Hallinan stating it remained "consistent with our original adjusted earnings guidance" (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan).

  • Revenue expectations shifted lower on a reported basis due to the CAM timing. Hallinan said, "We now anticipate total company revenue will be about flat compared to the last year, which is slightly lower than prior guidance because of the removal of CAM from the second quarter expectations" (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan), while maintaining that organic revenue is still expected to grow low single digits.

  • Management updated tariff and inflation framing versus its prior call assumptions. Hallinan said updated tariff policy changes were expected to be "net tailwind" on a gross basis versus beginning-of-year assumptions, but added, "We believe the combined impact from these inflationary pressures roughly offsets the benefit from the tariff tailwind in the year" (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan).

  • For Q2, Hallinan guided to net sales around $3.9 billion and adjusted EPS of approximately $1.15 to $1.25 (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan), and said Q2 adjusted gross margin improvement was expected to be about 300 basis points year-over-year.

Financial Results

  • Nelson said Q1 adjusted gross margin was 30.2% and adjusted EBITDA margin was 9.2% (President, CEO & Director Nelson). He also reported Tools & Outdoor revenue of approximately $3.3 billion (up 2% year-over-year) and Engineered Fastening revenue up 10% reported (7% organically) (President, CEO & Director Nelson).

  • In Engineered Fastening, Nelson said "The Aerospace business continued its strong performance, achieving 31% organic growth" and that Automotive delivered 4% organic growth, while general industrial fasteners declined low-single digits (President, CEO & Director Nelson).

  • Hallinan emphasized the bridge behind Q1 EPS and the CAM timing effect on segment profit. He said moving CAM out of Q2 lowered Engineered Fastening pretax profit by about $15 million, but "it also lowers second quarter interest expense by a similar amount, meaning it has essentially no impact on second quarter or full year adjusted EPS guidance" (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan).

Q&A

  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe Research: asked to unpack the gross margin step-up from first half to second half; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan said, "we can see the 34-and-a-fraction percentage gross margin for the third quarter already on our balance sheet" and attributed the step-up primarily to "net productivity benefits," "adjusting our fixed cost structure," and "ongoing tariff mitigation efforts."

  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe Research: asked if temporary IEEPA-related tariff relief creates a P&L benefit; President, CEO & Director Nelson said the benefit was viewed as temporary because "we do expect the 301s to be reinstated" and that tariff-related tailwinds were partly offset by inflation from battery metals and Middle East-driven inputs.

  • Julian Mitchell, Barclays: asked about Tools & Outdoor volumes; President, CEO & Director Nelson said underlying demand remained "relatively flattish," while reiterating an expected Q2 inflection tied to lapping prior-year promotional disruption.

  • Timothy Wojs, Baird: asked about relative POS after competitors took price; President, CEO & Director Nelson said management saw "more of an even playing field on pricing" and that SKU-level tracking showed performance in line with internal expectations.

  • Timothy Wojs, Baird: asked on CRAFTSMAN timing and sales/margins impact; President, CEO & Director Nelson said it is one of the company’s "largest-ever" new product launch cycles for the brand and that management expects "CRAFTSMAN momentum by year-end and certainly going into 2027."

  • Richard Reid, Wells Fargo: asked on USMCA and China tariff mitigation; President, CEO & Director Nelson said the company was "a little bit ahead of pace" on USMCA qualification progress and reiterated the intent to be "less than 5% of our sales in the U.S. coming from China-sourced product by the end of the year."

  • Aanvi Patodia, Goldman Sachs: asked on CAM divestiture puts/takes; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan reiterated the quarterly CAM contribution framework previously provided and said the Q2 profit reduction and interest expense reduction were "roughly equivalent."

  • Brett Linzey, Mizuho: asked on pro battery replacement cycle and innovation; President, CEO & Director Nelson said replacement cadence is more applicable to DIY, while pro demand is influenced by high-intensity use and jobsite re-tooling, and pointed to 20V XR, FLEXVOLT, and POWERSHIFT as key platforms.

  • Christopher Snyder, Morgan Stanley: asked about competitive risk from tariff rollbacks; President, CEO & Director Nelson said the company’s baseline expects 301s to replace IEEPA, and added it expects to be "at parity to probably mildly advantaged" as USMCA qualification increases.

  • Robert Wertheimer, Melius Research: asked about trends through April; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan said conditions through Q1 were "consistent with the back half of last year" and that guidance assumes buyers remain relatively steady, with cost structure actions if demand changes.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative, with repeated probing on the durability of margin expansion, tariff pass-through mechanics, competitive pricing risk, and end-demand stability, including questions about an "unstable" consumer backdrop (Robert Wertheimer, Melius Research) and tariff-driven competitive dynamics (Christopher Snyder, Morgan Stanley).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more detailed-but-controlled in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing execution and visibility into margin levers; Hallinan said, "we have every intent on hitting it" on margin progression and framed downside sensitivities as mainly sales declines or "some very big new spike in inflation" (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language remained consistent on long-term margin targets, but the Q&A focus shifted more heavily toward tariff-policy changes, near-term inflation in battery metals/tungsten, and the mechanics of the CAM timing impact.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 management emphasized outperformance versus its own Q1 guidance range and framed outdoor preseason execution as a key driver, while the prior quarter emphasized Q4 margin strength alongside softer-than-expected revenue and continued retail volume pressure.

  • Guidance structure was largely maintained quarter-over-quarter on full-year adjusted EPS ($4.90 to $5.70) and the long-term gross margin objective; however, Q1 updated total revenue expectations to about flat, which Hallinan explicitly linked to removing CAM’s expected Q2 contribution.

  • Analyst questioning moved from Q4’s focus on margin cadence and price elasticity to Q1’s focus on tariff-policy revisions (including 232/301/122 framing), competitive pricing implications, and the sustainability of professional and outdoor demand.

Risks and Concerns

  • Hallinan cited inflationary pressures tied to geopolitics and inputs, saying that since the Middle East conflict the company has seen "inflationary cost pressures in resins and freight" and "meaningful inflation" in "battery metals and tungsten" (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan).

  • Management described tariff outcomes as dependent on policy decisions. Hallinan said the company’s base case assumes new Section 301 tariffs are introduced at levels similar to the old IEEPA tariffs, while noting the expectation is "subject to change" (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Hallinan).

  • Nelson framed end-market risk as continued North American retail softness and flat underlying demand, while presenting mitigation through promotional refinements, professional-channel investments, and tariff mitigation initiatives (President, CEO & Director Nelson).

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 as an execution-led start to 2026, anchored by outdoor preseason performance and continued professional-channel momentum, while keeping full-year adjusted EPS guidance at $4.90 to $5.70. Leadership emphasized the CAM divestiture’s debt paydown and a pivot toward share repurchases under a $500 million authorization, alongside a margin roadmap targeting 35-plus percent adjusted gross margin by Q4 2026, even as tariff-policy uncertainty and inflation in resins, freight, battery metals, and tungsten remain key variables.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Rush Street rallies to an all-time high after strong earnings
data.description:
Rush Street Interactive (RSI) rallied to an all-time high on Wednesday after the company topped first-quarter earnings expectations.

Revenue was up 41.1% to a first quarter record of $370.36M. Notably, monthly active users rose 51% year over year to approximately 839K. MAUs in North America were up 46% during the quarter, driven by 62% growth in online casino markets. Average revenue per monthly active user in the United States and Canada was $317 during the quarter, while ARPMAU in Latin America was $54.

Weighing in on the report, Oppenheimer analyst Jed Kelly said the firm expects strong  LATAM growth for the balance of the year on easing comparables, the World Cup factor, and Colombia implementing GGR tax. "Players are gravitating to RSI's smart bonusing infrastructure that is equating to the industry's lowest churn and more predictable earnings algorithm, with a higher player base suggesting further upside to EBITDA," he added.

The firm raised its price target on Rush Street (RSI) to $30 from $25.

Shares of Rush Street Interactive (RSI) were up 16.1% at 12:22 p.m. to $27.87 and traded as high as $29 earlier in the session. The Seeking Alpha Quant Rating on RSI has been flashing Buy since last December.
